posts.text:
Can AI governance align with democratic principles? 🤖

Our new paper examines how social contract theory—from Hobbes to Rawls—breaks down when applied to opaque algorithms that decide welfare, policing, and hiring.

Published in AIES 2025 👇🧵

https://doi.org/10.1609/aies.v8i1.36575

The challenge: AI systems now make critical decisions affecting millions, yet they're often opaque, unaccountable, and centrally controlled.

How does this square with democratic legitimacy? Are we creating an "algorithmic Leviathan"? ⚖️

We turned to social contract theory—the framework behind modern governance—to examine AI legitimacy.

Classical theorists like Hobbes, Locke, and Rousseau assumed transparency and accountability. AI breaks these assumptions.

We identified three dimensions where AI governance fails:

✅ Procedural (transparency + participation)
✅ Moral-substantive (fairness + rights)
✅ Recognitional (including affected communities)

Current AI systems struggle on all fronts.

How do classical philosophers hold up against AI?

• Hobbes: Works for clear authority, fails with diffuse AI harms
• Locke: Requires "opt-out" options—impossible with pervasive algorithms
• Rousseau: Direct participation? Not at AI scale 📊

• Rawls: Assumes transparency for fairness—but algorithms lack clarity
• Nozick: Focuses on individual rights, misses collective harms like biased hiring models

Classical theories weren't built for opaque, global systems. 🌍

The solution? A hybrid social contract model that remixes classical ideas:

🔹 Hobbesian accountability
🔹 Lockean rights protection  
🔹 Rousseauian participation
🔹 Rawlsian fairness
🔹 Nozickian freedom from coercion

Practical fixes for AI governance:

• Citizen assemblies for algorithmic oversight
• Real-time transparency dashboards
• Global cooperation (AI doesn't respect borders)
• Participatory regulation of biased systems 🛠️

We also integrate critical perspectives—feminist and intersectional critiques—to address systemic discrimination in both historical institutions and algorithmic systems.

AI governance needs pluralism, not one-size-fits-all ethics. 🌈

Why this matters: Algorithms aren't just tools—they're rulers mediating access to justice, jobs, and resources.

Getting governance wrong risks deepening inequality and eroding democratic trust. @GRAIL_center @purduepolsci

Full paper: https://doi.org/10.1609/aies.v8i1.36575

For policy practitioners and governance teams working on algorithmic accountability—curious what principles you think are missing?

[Add coauthors] #AIGovernance #ResponsibleAI

[Optional: tag journal/outlet here]

linkedin.text:
Can AI governance align with democratic principles? Our new research published in AIES 2025 examines this critical question: https://doi.org/10.1609/aies.v8i1.36575

AI systems now make decisions affecting millions—determining welfare eligibility, policing strategies, and hiring outcomes. Yet these systems are often opaque, unaccountable, and centrally controlled. How does this square with democratic legitimacy?

➤ We turned to social contract theory—the philosophical framework behind modern governance—to examine AI's legitimacy crisis. Classical theorists from Hobbes to Rawls assumed transparency and accountability. AI breaks these fundamental assumptions.

✅ Our analysis reveals three dimensions where current AI governance fails:
• Procedural legitimacy (transparency + participation)
• Moral-substantive legitimacy (fairness + rights protection)  
• Recognitional legitimacy (including affected communities)

➤ Classical philosophers struggle with AI realities:
• Hobbes works for clear authority but fails with diffuse AI harms
• Locke requires "opt-out" options—impossible with pervasive algorithms
• Rousseau's direct participation doesn't scale to automated systems
• Rawls assumes transparency for fairness—algorithms lack clarity
• Nozick focuses on individual rights, missing collective harms

✅ Our solution: A hybrid social contract model blending Hobbesian accountability, Lockean rights protection, Rousseauian participation, Rawlsian fairness, and Nozickian freedom from coercion.

Practical implications include citizen assemblies for algorithmic oversight, real-time transparency dashboards, and global cooperation frameworks. We also integrate critical perspectives to address systemic discrimination in both historical and algorithmic institutions.

For policy practitioners and governance teams: What principles do you think are missing from current AI governance approaches?

[Add coauthors] [Add funder]

GRAIL Center | Purdue University

#AIGovernance #ResponsibleAI #AIethics #TechPolicy #AlgorithmicAccountability #SocialContract
